figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of the L-RFM construction and residual least-squares solve. The method forms local or global space–time trial functions from frozen liquid random features, evaluates closed-form responses and analytic derivatives, assembles the weighted collocation residual, and solves the linear least-squares readout problem.
Table 1: Matched-capacity rules for isolating trial-space effects.
Figure 2: Convergence of the 1D heat-equation solve under PoU and feature-count refinement. Curves and bands show mean ± sample standard deviation over five random seeds.
Figure 4: Temporal-scale resolution on a prescribed multi-scale temporal problem. Curves show five-seed mean relative L2 error versus P; shaded bands show sample standard deviation.

Figure 5: Accuracy and conditioning trends across the Allen–Cahn diffusion-scale sweep. Left: mean relative L2 error. Right: raw row-weighted LS condition number. Error bars show sample standard deviation over five random seeds.
Figure 6: Representative stiff Allen–Cahn solution field at ϵ = 10−4.

Figure 7: Ablations of the liquid temporal response and time-scale sampling law. Left: removing the ODE-driven temporal response on the onedimensional benchmark set. Right: replacing log-uniform τ/T ∈ [0.05, 20] by fixed τ/T ∈ {0.05, 1, 20} on Allen–Cahn. Bars show five-seed mean relative L2 error; error bars show sample standard deviation.

Figure 8: Three-dimensional prescribed temporal-scale problem solved by L-RFM-Local. Mid-plane slices at z = 0.5 are shown for one representative seed; columns show five time levels, and rows show the reference solution, L-RFM-Local solution, and absolute error.
Figure 9: Block marching controls long-time Allen–Cahn error over T = 2.0. Curves and bands show mean ± sample standard deviation over five random seeds.
Figure 10: Representative long-time Allen–Cahn solution from block marching.
Table B.8: Experimental configurations used for the main-text and supplementary results.

Table B.9: Supplementary error and conditioning results for selected one-dimensional benchmarks. Relative L∞ errors and empirical LS condition numbers are reported as mean ± sample standard deviation over five random seeds.
